Hey guys, welcome to module 7 and this is the final core subsequence of our launch or final ends, also the one where if done right, you can expect the largest percentage of your sales to roll in. However, it's also one of the most often misunderstood sequences and that's why if you're only going to come away with one thing in this entire program, I want it to be this. We are not selling urgency. You know, typically at this point, you'll just see kind of a barrage of warnings, you know, 48 hours, 24 hours, 12 hours, 8 hours, 6 hours, 2 hours. Oh, I swear I'm going to take it down. I'm sending you another email right now because if I don't, you'll be angry and just all sorts of desperate, sometimes cringe worthy, FOMO driven stuff. And the only reason this kind of sort of works for getting an uptick in sales is because while you're staying on their mind, your top of mind, you're relevant, you're in their inbox, there is that frequency and that just being present and vivid and they see it. And the other one is yes, of course, urgency and FOMO does work to a certain degree. But if they haven't bought by this point, well, there may be some other stuff to coach, to get them across. And any success I've had as a marketer has come not by asking why 3% of prospects bought, but by asking why 97% of prospects didn't. Because that's where the gold is and that's where we're going to plant our shovels right now and start digging. So here we go. If we're not selling urgency, then what are we selling? If your launcher funnel has built-in time urgency with, you know, a defined close date, then the time urgency will already be a naturally occurring factor. You know, you could even have a countdown timer achieving that goal, but your job as a human, as a human who has empathy is to assess and communicate the more subtle precursors that will make a prospect say yes. So if we're not selling that surface level urgency, or at least solely relying on that urgency, then what are we actually selling? And this has been the biggest game changer for me. We're selling decisiveness. Because when you really think about it, the reason most people don't buy isn't because they decided not to, but because they didn't decide at all. And while time urgency certainly does play a role in getting people to decide it's by no means the only thing or even the most powerful thing. And our job here is to not ditch surface level urgency entirely, but to build upon it, to hit those precursors right below the surface that support your prospects ability to make a decision, which in turn will amplify our ability to convert into closing days and hours of the sequence. So while the main thing we're coaching here is decisiveness, how do we actually coach it? You know, and a good place to start is to counter the things that impair or get in the way of decision making. And on the surface level of that, you know, one of the main reasons people don't make decisions is either too much information or not enough information. And that's why, as we mentioned in a previous module, we're very careful not to start throwing more and more sales arguments against the wall and overwhelming them to the point of decision paralysis. And the same goes for, you know, too many pricing or packaging options. Barry Schwartz, no relation has a book called the paradox of choice, which really warms us as marketers not to overwhelm the customer with options and information. So the main takeaway here is to really avoid building too many extra sales arguments and to not have more than two or max three packages or pricing options. But what I really want to focus on here, which is in line with coaching the conversion is a little deeper, a little more subcutaneous, you know, it's those hidden factors that either hinder or encourage decision making. And that's what we're here to coach. So how to coach decisiveness. I like to start here by acknowledging and removing cognitive bias. You know, there might be some unconscious objections that prospects might have. Maybe they took an action or an action in the past, they feel shame, guilt or remorse around and buying your product would just be too confronting. So starting with the question, what does my prospect have to forgive themselves for, you know, to really, really open themselves up to this product, you know, and just asking this can open up a flood of really interesting insights for you. So that's one starting point. Another one you've seen it earlier, it comes back again. We are coaching exclusive empowerment. And just like how this was a crucial trigger for getting your prospect to take that milestone action that brought them onto the journey, it also bookends the buying journey. And just like how, you know, this principal nearly converted me into a three year, $100,000 investment into a law program. I didn't even want to get into it's an even better way to motivate people to decide when that decision actually correlates with cashing in on their privileged points in a way they want to in a way that really aligns with their context and the world and reality they want to live in. So the FOMO around leaving exclusivity points on the table can even be more powerful than then the FOMO of missing out on your product or the outcome it provides. You really want to leverage that, give people a chance to cash in on those privileged points. The third thing we're coaching is identification with a buyer. We're continuing this fight. We did it in the last module. We're going to really ramp it up here and really coach this aspect right until the end. Because the more we can get your prospect to identify with being a buyer, the easier it is for them to make a decision that feels natural and aligned with who and what they identify as. However, here is the important note around this. You know, we have this habit of setting the bar of being a buyer way too high. You know, like we talked about in the anatomy of a buyer lesson in module two, part of what we have to do is make the idea or makeup of a buyer more accessible. You know, lower the bar in the sense that they don't need to be a million percent confident. You know, they just have to be a hundred percent committed to giving it an honest shot. And the more specific you can be about what that shot looks like, the better. But instead, you know, we have this tendency to set the bar too high to be cleared. You know, and one thing that really ticks me off in this is we procrastinate. You know, we procrastinate. We make these claims. You know, we say we only want to work with action takers who are 110 percent committed. And while it's good to hold them to that aspiration, here's the thing. The action takers have already taken action. They bought the moment you said go. They bought five days ago when you opened cart and by procrastinating and setting the bar to only welcoming customers who are 110 percent confident and committed, you're in turn excluding most of your potential buyers, buyers who could be a great fit and get the result. If just given the chance and the right coaching at this stage of your funnel. So your job isn't to raise the bar and taunt them when they can't clear it. It's to lower it to a point where they can and then doing everything you can to help them rise into something greater on the other side, whatever that looks like in your program or business. So in this stage to coach decisiveness and help your prospect identify as a buyer, we lower the bar a little bit. We make it inclusive. We say that it's okay if they have their doubts, hesitation and resistance, and that's not a barrier to entry. You know, all they need is a minimum viable commitment. And this is how we set the bar to a level they can clear, where we give them a chance to express courage and commitment in a way that's accessible with where they're currently at. Instead of shaming or guilting them for their lack of ironclad confidence. So what we're doing is we're lowering lowering the bar to raise commitment. And a quick example that I want to take you through this is to just ask you what feels more inviting, you know, only join 10 X launches. If you're 110% committed to putting the strategies you learn into immediate excuse free action, or we get it. You have a rough relationship with copywriting for far too long to a thousand and 10% believe you can turn into the second coming of Ogilvy overnight. Yes, even with the help of two strange Canadians who've secretly written for half of the world's online launches in the last five years. And to that we say, cool, you know, BYOB, bring your own doubts and let Ryan I broker a peace deal once and for all. Let us give you your log in deeds. And if after poking around a few lessons this weekend and firing off a few theme plays, you don't get a sense of holy crap. I think I'm starting to get it just fire us an email or text and we'll do the whole refund dance. No harm, no foul. So think about that for a second. The reason the second one feels better is because it comes back down to a core principle of coaching, which is empathy, no validating where your prospect is at and making that okay, instead of creating this unrealistic caricature of something they have trouble projecting themselves into. Now this isn't to say we don't create a high standard for the type of customer we want to work with, but it does mean that we leave room for natural human behavior, which for most of us includes anxiety, uncertainty and skepticism. And you should look at your own buying experience for this. Are you 110% sure all the time? Do you ever experience anxiety, hesitation or resistance? And if not, you know, if you, if not, if you actually do experience these things, why are you setting the bar so unrealistically high for your audience? You know, no one is 100% confident about anything. No one's 100% confident they'll be alive tomorrow that the person they love will be around forever. So why should they be 100% confident in what you're selling? And their ability to make the most of it. So at the end of the day, the best way to overcome is to include. We take the path of least resistance by offering your audience the minimum viable commitment while deeply acknowledging and reflecting back exactly how they're feeling in that moment and making that feeling congruent with a buyer experience. And in doing so, give them the most frictionless path beyond the buying line. And here is what I like to reference as a buyer, you know, they're allowed to bring doubt, skepticism, fear and resistance, but just as long as confidence, trust and excitement outweighs it, then you're going to get the sale. Now, the flip side of, you know, the minimum viable commitment is the quick and valuable win. This is where you really need to think about the ownership experience of your product and, you know, how this ties into how you've designed your course itself. But for the purposes of marketing or program, it's super important to identify what not only the quickest one would be, but the quickest and most valuable win. Now, what element of your pro program can your prospect dive into literally hours within getting their login or access information and generate an important outcome or solve one of the most painful elements of the problem. So it may not be the final culmination of your program. But it's such that even if that's all they got, you know, after minutes or hours of using it, it would make it well worth the while. You know, and quick wins is a concept I've heard before from Stanford researcher BJ Fogg and Danny Eany from Miracy as a, you know, as it relates to online course creation. But what this is, is it's essentially used to enhance user experience and build momentum in the early stages of your course. But to take this beyond instructional design and actually leverage the concept to coach decisiveness and increase conversions, the quick and valuable win is something we need to take a deeper look at. You know, the quick and valuable win has real world tangible value. You know, it's worth something and it's something that they could achieve within minutes. There's an ROI there. It addresses an aspect of the moment of highest tension. You know, it's a very quick acting pain reliever. You know, there's that immediate pain relief or pleasure provider, you know, depending on what you're doing. And there's that instant gratification or relief. You know, this isn't just a quick, this isn't just a quick, crunchy win that they can achieve within minutes of receiving your product, but it also has tangible real world value to it. It addresses some aspect of their moment of highest tension and it may not produce the ultimate and final outcome, but it acts as a much needed hit of morphine for your prospects, most urgent problem or a highly practical and immediate win. That's an important piece to the final outcome. And this is important. That is Mount Everest. And just like how, I mean, anyone would be crazy to climate, but you'd be more likely to take that 10 week perilous journey up Everest. If you knew that on day two, you'd hit a majestic ridge, you know, that legend holds possesses an eluxer of youth and vitality. You know, so sure the two week journey is important to you and aspirational and might have this like lifelong goal and outcome that you've been trying to achieve, but you'd be a lot more likely to go on that journey. If there was a valuable and quick and tangible reward, you know, on day two or a few days in, this is also congruent. What, with what world class performance coach, Todd Herman says about our horizon line for seeing outcomes, you know, while your course or program may promise these amazing and meaningful outcomes in three to four months from now, the reality of that outcome is just too far off in the distance for your prospect to reel in and identify with and ground in their reality. So it's important from a marketing and decisiveness perspective to reel that horizon line and ownership experience to the next few hours or days, you know, the further out you future pace, the more blurry that is and the more blurry it is, the less real and vivid it is in their life. You know, so you really want to focus on this quick win. That's also valuable, the highest possible ROI that you can deliver the new approach and paradigm towards writing copy, which we call coaching the conversion. We also offer that immediate quick, valuable win of diving into whatever phase of your launch you need the most help with, you know, most likely going to be in the sales and closing sequences and applying one of the theme plates included. So this is something which can and has generated two X, five X or even 10 X ROI and literally a few hours providing you are the right fit for the program and exclusively empowered to get those results. And another example would be a meditation program for entrepreneurs. You know, if you're selling a mindfulness or meditation program to frantic entrepreneurs who have trouble disconnecting and live with this constant tension and anxiety, sure, over the course of four, six, 12 weeks, you'll ground them in a totally new way of experiencing life, a flipped paradigm where they'll be able to relate to situations with more clarity of mind, peacefulness and resourcefulness. But if they're a moment of highest tension, they're bleeding, they're bleeding moment, you know, is that they're constantly afraid of failure or messing up and they have this crazy anxiety that stops them from being present with their family than maybe the quick, valuable win here is a bank of, you know, special, short guided meditations that promise to dissolve and relax that within 10 minutes, you know, so that immediate pain killer. And that's the QVW, you know, so in the workbook, in the next section, we're going to walk you through that, how to uncover that, how to actually mark down what that is for your program and to offer that and to really leverage that in your closing sequence. The next thing we're coaching here is binary choice. You know, to coach decisiveness, we need to make sure that our prospect is aware that a binary choice exists, that a choice does indeed exist and must be made. And this is arguably the biggest surface level reason why personal selling with follow ups closes 10x higher than online strategies because it requires decision, you know, they can't just remove themselves from the context without making clear and conscious decision that yes, they want to move forward or no, not for me versus the online world where they can choose not to choose, you know, let the deadline pass them by and then smooth their like FOMO wounds with rationalizations around why I wasn't a good fit later, you know, and while we can't force a decision via online marketing, we can definitely encourage and incentivize one. No, from a marketing perspective, it's far more powerful to have someone take a negative action like saying no, then doing nothing at all. No gives us insight, especially if we ask why no allows for the continuation of a conversation, but silence doesn't. So in the workbook, you'll find a few. Theme plates that addresses head on and coach people into knowing and realizing that there's indeed a choice to be made and a responsibility to themselves to make one, which leads to the next thing we're coaching, which is control, collaboration and co-creation. Cause our prospect will be more likely to acknowledge choice and their responsibility to choose if they feel a sense of control around it. You know, we resist when we feel pushed. So it's not a matter of telling them they must choose now, but instead giving them the conditions in which they feel a sense of power to do it. And this works especially well if they've had a hand in the thing they're choosing. So this is where co-creation comes into play. You know, for example, if you take their pulse 24 hours before the close and ask them, what do you need to make a decision or what's stopping you? And then slightly tailor your clothes to address that missing link. They'll feel less like you're pushing an offer them and more like they're collaborating with you to make it work. Now, of course, this isn't the easiest thing to do at scale, but in the workbook, you'll see a theme plate that works to leverage this principle, the essence of it, and empower your prospect to co-create a close. And in doing so, feel more empowered to take ownership over the decision. And then here's a big one, second hand FOMO. And this is another tool I used to coach decisiveness, you know, making the decision more interpersonal, you know, it's no longer just their FOMO, it's our fear of them missing out. You know, how their decision or lack thereof can affect other stakeholders, you know, and this is important. This is really big when we could share our own fear of them missing out, reminding them of why you do what you do, you know, what breaks your heart and how can they be part of helping that? And you'll see a theme plate later as well that will really dive in deeper, you know, how their decision affects other objects and things and how it's not just about them. And the point here is to show them that the consequences of indecisiveness goes beyond their own concern. And it gives them a chance to be more responsible towards those other factors. So when we stop making the decision all about them, we actually start channeling their empathy to feel that their decision is part of a bigger whole, which can really encourage conscious decision making. So those are really the precursors, the stuff beneath the iceberg that we're really going to be working on and coaching through the final 48 hours of your launch. But before we dive into the workbook to tackle this part, this crucial part of your sequence, here is another final really big one. It is the risk reversal. It's the safety and the outcomes and your ability to deliver it. And in the sale sequence, we introduced the risk revealer where we brought to light, kind of how the cost of not moving forwards is greater than moving forward. But here we need to bring total safety to that choice, you know, safety and the outcomes and your ability to deliver it. And when you do this, the combination of the risk revealer and risk reversal, that becomes risk mitigation. You know, the culmination of revealing the risk and then reversing it frames the buying decision as one that mitigates overall risk. And that's ultimately what makes people feel good about making a choice. So yes, we use surface level urgency and time urgency, but we also get them deeply attuned to the real urgency that comes through both revealing risk and framing the decision as a way to mitigate it, which kind of lends itself to a technique I call pain anchoring. You know, it's like price anchoring. We've heard that term before where one option becomes exponentially more desirable when compared with the other. And I'll show you a little later how to anchor the pain around indecision versus the pain in missing out completely. This could be really powerful. We're going to explore some theme plates to utilize it, but I want to stop this now. I know we've covered a lot and it's quite a bit for just this small little bit of a little 48 hour window. But since this is where the bulk of your sales are going to come, you know, if you have an evergreen funnel and plug in emails that support these principles, that is going to be automated revenue for months or years to come. So it's really important that even though this is a small sequence, we really address the true factors that make people decide, you know, if decisiveness is the main thing that separates one on one selling with follow ups, you know, that converts 10 times higher by just getting our prospects to a place where they could decide, you know, make it a very clear binary choice and giving them the tools and coaching them around all the things that enhance their ability to do so. This is going to be a game changer. So I'm going to leave you here. I'm going to meet you back in the workbook where we'll dive into some questions to consider. And then we'll dive even deeper into a series of theme plates that will activate and leverage all these principles we just discussed and really take the guesswork out of it. So that's it. I really, really appreciate you being here and sticking through all these concepts and watch it again if you have to. But if you're ready, I will meet you in the workbook. I'll see you there.
